OlRjTCIAAAAJ.keywords: Virtualization, GPU/accelerator virtualization, Non-volatile/persistent memory (NVM/PMEM), Heterogeneous memory management, Datacenter energy and thermal management, Edge computing, Edge-to-cloud continuum, Secure NFV and SGX, Checkpointing and fault tolerance, Resource allocation and scheduling (VMs/containers)
OlRjTCIAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Ada Gavrilovska is a faculty researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where she leads a research group working at the intersection of *systems software*, *distributed systems*, and *edge/cloud infrastructure*. Her work focuses on enabling high-performance, secure, and energy-efficient computing across heterogeneous platforms by advancing virtualization, accelerator support, and persistent memory integration. Central themes in her research include <u>GPU and accelerator virtualization</u>, <u>heterogeneous memory management</u> (including byte-addressable non-volatile memory), and the design of software and OS support for the <u>edge-to-cloud continuum</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Ada Gavrilovska’s body of work spans several tightly connected research areas. She has made foundational contributions to *virtualization and hypervisor design* for both high-performance computing and cloud environments, addressing I/O service differentiation, power metering of VMs, and hypervisor architectures for multicore and cluster systems (e.g., work on VM power metering, high-performance hypervisors, and I/O scheduling). A second major thread is *accelerator support and GPU sharing* in virtualized/cloud environments — exemplified by GViM and GPUShare — that enable multi-tenant GPU use and GPU-accelerated virtual machines. A third area is *non-volatile and heterogeneous memory systems*: Gavrilovska’s papers on NoveLSM, pVM, Phoenix, and related works explore persistent LSM-based stores, persistent virtual memory, and memory-speed HPC I/O using NVM, including checkpointing techniques tuned for NVM. Complementing these are contributions to *datacenter energy and thermal management*, *edge computing and function onloading*, and *secure state handling for NFV using trusted execution environments (SGX)*. Across these topics she emphasizes practical system designs that balance performance, isolation, persistence, and manageability for modern datacenter, HPC, and edge deployments.

---

## Notable Works
- "GViM: GPU-accelerated virtual machines" (2009, 363 citations) — introduced mechanisms to enable efficient access to GPUs from virtual machines, demonstrating how virtualization can be reconciled with accelerator-driven high-performance workloads and enabling multi-tenant GPU use in shared environments.  
- "Redesigning LSMs for nonvolatile memory with NoveLSM" (2018, 269 citations) — presented NoveLSM, a persistent LSM-based key-value store that leverages byte-addressable NVM, direct mutability of persistent state, and opportunistic parallelism to achieve low-latency, high-throughput persistence for applications.  
- "HeteroOS: OS Design for Heterogeneous Memory Management in Datacenter" (2017, 187 citations) — proposed OS-level approaches for managing heterogeneous memory in virtualized datacenters, addressing page hotness tracking, migration, and the limits of hypervisor-only solutions to improve application performance on mixed memory substrates.

## Academic Background
Ada Gavrilovska is a senior faculty member in the College of Computing at the Georgia Institute of Technology and maintains an active research homepage (http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~ada). Over a sustained career she has published extensively in premier systems, networking, and HPC venues and has accumulated over 4,800 citations across her papers. Her scholarship is distinguished by cross-cutting contributions to virtualization, accelerator-enabled computing, and the integration of non-volatile memories into system software. Gavrilovska’s research has been supported through competitive grants and industry collaborations, and she is an active contributor to the systems research community through conference program committees, peer review, and graduate student mentorship.
